description: A material or surface is 'wuloe' when it yields readily to pressure or cushions contact. The word can also describe a physically soft landing. By contrast, 'moli' (gentle) belongs to manner and temperament; 'wuloe' stays with yielding texture and physical contact.
articulatory_notes: Rounded lips and a raised back tongue form 'w' and continue directly into high-back 'u'. The tongue tip then contacts the alveolar ridge for 'l' and releases into rounded mid-back 'o'. Stress rests on 'lo'; final true-mid front 'e' remains a separate syllable after the hiatus.
sound_symbolism: The rounded opening of 'wuloe' passes through lateral 'l' without a stop. Its yielding contour offers a small mnemonic for a surface that gives under pressure.
usage_notes: The phrase 'wuloe shumu' describes soft moss. The corpus also places 'wuloe' before 'lepa' (fall) for a soft landing, where bodily contact remains the source of the image. A kind action or restrained voice takes 'moli' (gentle).

semantic_domains.physical: a yielding texture or contact that cushions pressure
